column7:
Of course not. But by walking down the road, that is the risk you are taking. Every action we have has a risk associated with it, no matter how unlikely. 

Disclosing to a potential sexual partner that you are transgender is an incredibly difficult thing for a trans person to do and can certainly put them in physical harm. A 2013 report from the National Coalition of Anti-Violence Programs found that 72% of victims of anti-LGBT homicide were transgender women. The same report found that trans people are "7 times as likely to experience physical violence when interacting with the police" than cisgender victims and survivors. Trans people are 1.7 times more likely to be the victims of sexual violence than cisgender people. [Source] (http://avp.org/storage/documents/2013_ncavp_hvreport_final.pdf)

One trans person is murdered every three days. Wikipedia keeps a running list. [Source] (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_unlawfully_killed_transgender_people).

Sitting at home on your computer, it's easy to tell trans people that they should disclose their surgery to any sexual partners. In practice, it's an incredibly sensitive issue that greatly increases said person's risk of sexual violence and harassment.
And you have a chance at getting raped walking down the road, that does not mean it is ok.

@achromatic-cathode, that's a false equivalence. Getting raped while walking down the road is not the same as a trans person choosing to disclose their status to a partner. One is a random act of violence, the other is a deliberate decision that can have serious consequences. You're not considering the power dynamics and risks involved in disclosure for trans people.

"@achromatic-cathode and @funny-acceleration, let's keep the discussion respectful and focused on the topic. @funny-acceleration, your points about the risks and challenges faced by trans individuals are valid and appreciated. @achromatic-cathode, your concern about safety is also understood. Let's avoid comparing different situations and instead work towards a deeper understanding of the complexities involved. How can we foster a supportive and informed conversation about disclosure and safety for all individuals, particularly those in the trans community?"

column7:
Word 1: designed to describe a phenomenon when a specific man is assumedly being condescending and sexist

Word 2: designed to generalize an entire race

Not even close to the same thing.

I elsewhere used the analogy of "white supremacy." As a white person, I don't feel indicted when someone says "white supremacy," because I'm not a white supremacist. Having the word "white" in the name doesn't imply that it's universal among white people. Or, to use a gendered one, when Rush says "feminazi" he's not condemning all women, even though "fem" is right there in the name.

To be honest, though, I tend to just call it *'splaining* just to avoid the issue you bring up -- plus it has the additional benefit of covering all such acts regardless of whether they're rooted in sexism, racism, or other prejudice.

@juvenile-layout, nice try, but you're still missing the point. Just because you don't feel indicted by the term "white supremacy" doesn't mean others won't take offense to a term that generalizes an entire group. And by the way, "feminazi" is a pretty loaded term itself, so not sure that's the best example to use. And what's with the '*splaining*'? You think that's a neutral term? Come on.

"@ternary-measure and @juvenile-layout, let's keep the discussion respectful and focused on the topic. @juvenile-layout, your point about the term 'white supremacy' is understood, but @ternary-measure also has a valid concern about how terms can be perceived. Let's avoid loaded language and examples that might derail the conversation. @juvenile-layout, can you clarify what you mean by '*splaining*' and how it relates to the topic at hand?"

column7:
> "To put this in perspective, the top 1 percent of taxpayers pay an average nationwide effective tax rate of just 5.4 percent."

this is a lie.  I have no idea where they got that number, but it's utterly wrong.  The [richest 1% pay about 30%](https://www.cbo.gov/sites/default/files/113th-congress-2013-2014/reports/49440-Distribution-of-Income-and-Taxes-2.pdf)  the people doing the report cannot be ignorant enough to get that number so wrong, which can only mean that they are absurdly absusing statistics to prove something they wanted to prove.  the whole report should be chucked.

>Millionaires pay 30% on taxable wages,

No, they pay 40%, on top of payroll and other taxes.

>After that it's mostly capital gains, which are taxed at 15%. 

No, it isn't.  capital gains is less than 1/3 of the income of the top 1%.  please read the CBO report I provided rather than just making shit up.

>In addition, the CBO is not counting non-taxable corporate income, since that inome does not "belong" to the person,

yes, they are, read the damned report.

>So yes, a 5.4 percent effective tax rate is entirely possible. 

No, it isn't.

>Warren Buffet only pays 17%, and he makes no efforts to lower his tax burden by shifting holdings out of his name nor out of the country.

warren buffet makes massive charitable donations, and takes the tax deduction for them.

@rigid-plain, I see you're still not getting it. The CBO report you provided actually supports my point, not yours. The top 1% do pay around 30% on taxable wages, but that's not the whole story. When you factor in capital gains and other forms of income, the effective tax rate can be much lower. And yes, Warren Buffett's tax rate is around 17%, which is a great example of how the tax system can be navigated to minimize liability. Maybe instead of just reading the report, you should try to understand the nuances of tax law and how it applies to different types of income.

"@rigid-plain and @poached-binary, let's keep the discussion civil and focused on the topic. @poached-binary, your point about considering multiple forms of income is valid, but @rigid-plain also raises important questions about the accuracy of the initial claim. Let's avoid making assumptions and personal remarks, and instead, work together to understand the data and its implications."

column7:
>It's not reasonable to always ask if you are allowed to hug someone, before you do it. 

Why? It takes 5 seconds. If you have the uncontrollable urge to hug people maybe you should talk to a therapist.

It doesnt matter what i feel, it matters how they feel...

Did you continue to go in for a hug if they recoiled, do you give them the chance to do so. The fact that you dont care what the other person feels is why that person said you need a therapist.

You seem to not think hugs are a big deal. But other people do. The fact that youre only concerned with yourself makes your hugs selfish.

@poached-binary, are you kidding me? You think a 5-second ask-to-hug protocol is too much to ask for? What's next, just going around groping people because "it's just a hug"? @ternary-measure nailed it, it's not about your urge to hug, it's about respecting the other person's boundaries. Get some empathy, dude.

"@poached-binary, @ternary-measure, and @isomorphic-canon, let's keep the discussion respectful. @poached-binary, your point about the time it takes to ask is noted, but others have raised valid concerns about respecting people's boundaries.
